content: The complex number $z$ satisfying $z+|z|$ $=1+7 i$, then the value of $|z|^2$ equals
explanation:
Let $z=x+i y$

So, $z+|z|=x+i y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=1+7 i$

$$\Rightarrow\left(x+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\right)+i y=1+7 i$$

By comparing both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & x+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=1 \text { and } y=7 \\ \Rightarrow & x+\sqrt{x^2+(7)^2}=1 \\ \Rightarrow & x+\sqrt{x^2+49}=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{x^2+49}=1-x \\ \Rightarrow & x^2+49=1+x^2-2 x \\ \Rightarrow & \\ \Rightarrow & 2 x=-48 \Rightarrow x=-24 \end{array}$$

Therefore, $z=-24+7 i$

$$\begin{aligned} & |z| & =\sqrt{(-24)^2+(7)^2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & |z|^2 & =576+49=625 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$z_1, z_2$$ and $$z_3$$ are the vertices $$A, B$$ and $$C$$ respectively of an isosceles right angled triangle with right angled at $$C$$, then $$\left(z_1-z_3^{\prime}\right)\left(z_2-z_3\right)$$ equals to
explanation:
$$\because \angle C=90^{\circ}$$,

$$\therefore \quad A C=B C \quad\{\because \triangle A B C$$ is isosceles $$\}$$

So, $$\frac{z_2-z_3}{z_1-z_3}=\frac{B C}{A C} e^{i \pi / 2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\left(z_2-z_3\right)}{\left(z_1-z_3\right)}=i \quad \{\because B C=A C\}\\ & \Rightarrow\left(z_2-z_3\right)=i\left(z_1-z_3\right) \end{aligned}$$

Squaring on both sides,

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(z_2-z_3\right)^2=i^2\left(z_1-z_3\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow z_2^2+z_3^2-2\left(z_2 \cdot z_3\right)=-z_1^2-z_3^2+2\left(z_1 \cdot z_3\right) \\ & \Rightarrow z_1^2+z_2^2-2\left(z_1 \cdot z_2\right)=2\left(z_1 \cdot z_3\right)+2\left(z_2 \cdot z_3\right) \\ & -2\left(z_1 \cdot z_2\right)-2\left(z_3^2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(z_1-z_2\right)^2=2\left[z_1\left(z_3-z_2\right)-z_3\left(z_3-z_2\right)\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left(z_1-z_2\right)^2=2\left[\left(z_1-z_3\right)\left(z_3-z_2\right)\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\left(z_1-z_2\right)^2}{2}=\left(z_1-z_3\right)\left(z_3-z_2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow-\frac{\left(z_1-z_2\right)^2}{2}=\left(z_1-z_3\right)\left(z_2-z_3\right) \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\alpha$$ is a non -real fifth root of unity, then the value of $$3^{\left|1+\alpha+\alpha^2+\alpha^{-2}-\alpha^{-1 \mid}\right|}$$, is

If $$\alpha, \beta$$ and $$\gamma$$ are the cube roots of $$P,(P<0)$$, then for any $$x, y$$ and $$z$$ which does not make denominator zero, the expression $$\frac{x \alpha+y \beta+z \gamma}{x \beta+y \gamma+z \alpha}$$ equals to
explanation:
We have, $$x^3=p \Rightarrow x=p^{1 / 3}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad x=p^{1 / 3}, p^{1 / 3} \omega, p^{1 / 3} \omega^2$$

Let $$\alpha=p^{1 / 3}, \beta=p^{1 / 3} \omega, \gamma=p^{1 / 3} \omega^2$$

Now, $$\frac{x \alpha+y \beta+z \gamma}{x \beta+y \gamma+z \alpha}=\frac{x+y \omega+\omega^2 z}{x \omega+y \omega^2+z}=\frac{1}{\omega}=\omega^2$$

If $$\alpha=p^{1 / 3} \omega, \beta=p^{1 / 3}$$ and $$\gamma=p^{1 / 3} \omega^2$$, then

$$\frac{x \alpha+y \beta+z \gamma}{x \beta+y \gamma+z \alpha}=\frac{x \omega+y+z \omega^2}{x+y \omega^2+z \omega}=\frac{1}{\omega^2}=\omega$$

content: If $$x+\frac{1}{x}=1$$ and $$p=x^{4000}+\frac{1}{x^{4000}}$$ and $$q$$ is the digit at unit place in the number $$2^{2 n}+1$$, then the value of $$(p+q)$$ is equal to
explanation:
We have,

$$x+1 / x=1 \Rightarrow x^2-x+1=0 \therefore x=-\omega,-\omega^2$$

Now, for $$x=-\omega$$

$$p=\omega^{4000}+\frac{1}{\omega^{4000}}=\omega+\frac{1}{\omega}=-1$$

Similarly, for $$x=-\omega^2, p=-1$$ and for $$n>1$$,

$$2^n=4 k \therefore 2^{2 n}=2^{4 k}$$

$$=(16)^k=a$$ number with last digit 6

$$\Rightarrow \quad q=6+1=7$$

Hence, $$p+q=-1+7=6$$

content: Let $$z_k=\cos \left(\frac{2 k \pi}{10}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{2 k \pi}{10}\right) ; k=1,2, \ldots \ldots \ldots$$ 9, then $$\frac{1}{10}\left\{\left|1-z_1\right|\left|1-z_2\right| \ldots .\left|1-z_a\right|\right\}$$ equals to
explanation:
Clearly, $$1, z_1, z_2, \ldots \ldots z_9$$ are 10th roots of unity

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore z^{10}-1=(z-1)\left(z-z_1\right)\left(z-z_2\right) \ldots \ldots\left(z-z_9\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(z-z_1\right)\left(z-z_2\right) \ldots\left(z-z_9\right)=\frac{z^{10}-1}{z-1} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(z-z_1\right)\left(z-z_2\right) \ldots .\left(z-z_9\right)=1+z+z^2+\ldots z^9 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(1-z_1\right)\left(1-z_2\right) \ldots\left(1-z_9\right)=10 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{10}\left|1-z_1\right|\left|1-z_2\right| \ldots\left|1-z_9\right|=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: The condition in order that $$Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$$ are vertices of an isosceles triangle right angled at $$z_2$$, is
explanation:
Let $$P\left(Z_1\right), Q\left(Z_2\right)$$ and $$R\left(Z_3\right)$$ be the vertices an isosceles triangle $$P Q R$$ right angled at $$Q$$. Let us rotate $$Q\left(Z_2\right)$$ about $$P$$ in anti-clockwise direction, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & Z_3-Z_1=\frac{\left|Z_3-Z_1\right|}{\left|Z_2-Z_1\right|}\left(Z_2-Z_1\right) e^{\frac{i \pi}{4}} \\ & Z_3-Z_1=\frac{P R}{P Q}\left(Z_2-Z_1\right) e^{\frac{i \pi}{4}}=\sqrt{2}\left(Z_2-Z_1\right) e^{\frac{i \pi}{4}} \ldots \text { (i) } \end{aligned}$$

Also by rotating $$Q\left(Z_2\right)$$ about $$R\left(Z_3\right)$$ in clockwise direction.

$$\begin{aligned} Z_1-Z_3 & =\frac{\left|Z_1-Z_3\right|}{\left|Z_2-Z_3\right|}\left(Z_2-Z_3\right) e^{-i \frac{\pi}{4}} \\ \quad \quad \quad \quad Z_1-Z_3 & =\frac{P R}{Q R}\left(Z_2-Z_3\right) e^{-i \frac{\pi}{4}} \\ & =\sqrt{2}\left(Z_2-Z_3\right) e^{-i \frac{\pi}{4}}\quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Multiply Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(Z_3-Z_1\right)\left(Z_1-Z_3\right)=2\left(Z_2-Z_1\right)\left(Z_2-Z_3\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(Z_3-Z_1\right)^2=2\left(Z_1-Z_2\right)\left(Z_2-Z_3\right) \end{aligned}$$

Which is the required condition.

content: If $$(1+i)(2 i+1)(1+3 i) \ldots(1+n i)=x+i y$$, then $$2 \cdot 5 \cdot 10 \ldots\left(1+n^2\right)$$ is equal to
explanation:
Given,

$$(1+i)(2 i+1)(1+3 i) \ldots(1+n i)=x+i y \quad \text{.... (i)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \because\left(a_1+i b_1\right)\left(a_2+i b_2\right) \ldots\left(a_n+i b_n\right)=A+i B \\ & \Rightarrow r_1 \cdot r_2 \ldots r_n\left[\cos \left(\theta_1+\theta_2+\ldots+\theta_n\right)+i\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2+....+\theta_n)\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & r_1 \cdot r_2 \ldots r_n=\sqrt{A^2+B^2} \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \text { and } & \tan \left(\theta_1+\theta_2+\ldots+\theta_n\right)=\frac{B}{A} \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{array}$$

Now, from Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{1^2+1^2} \cdot \sqrt{2^2+1^2} \cdot \sqrt{3^2+1^2} \ldots \sqrt{1^2+n^2} \\ & =\sqrt{x^2+y^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{10} \ldots \sqrt{1+n^2}=\sqrt{x^2+y^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 10 \ldots\left(1+n^2\right)=x^2+y^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The non-zero solutions of the equation $$z^2+|z|=0$$, where $$z$$ is a complex number, are
explanation:
Given equation is $$z^2+|z|=0\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Let $$\quad z=x+i y$$

So that, $$(x+i y)^2+|x+i y|=0 \quad$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad x^2-y^2+2 i x y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(x^2-y^2+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\right)+i(2 x y)=0 \end{aligned}$$

On equating real and imaginary parts, we get

$$x^2-y^2+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=0 \quad \text{.... (ii)}$$

and $$2 x y=0 \Rightarrow x=0$$ or $$y=0\quad \text{.... (iii)}$$

If $$(x=0)$$, then from Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & -y^2 \pm y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y( \pm 1-y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=0 \text { or } y= \pm 1 \text {. } \\ \end{aligned}$$

If $$(y=0)$$, then from Eq. (ii), we get

$$x^2 \pm x=0$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \Rightarrow & x(x \pm 1) =0 \\ \Rightarrow & x =0 \text { or } x= \pm 1 \end{array}$$

Hence, the non-zero solutions are $$\pm i$$ and $$\mp 1$$.
